seoTitle: Lower Back Pain After Sex: Why It Happens, How to Fix
seoDescription: Back hurts after sex? It is usually a load-and-posture story, not a punishment. The honest why, the boring fixes, and when to see someone.
h1: Lower Back Pain After Sex: The Honest Why (and the Boring Fixes)
dek: If your back is fine in the moment and furious the next morning, that is a clue, not a curse. The pain that shows up later is almost always about how long your spine sat at the edge of its range, and in which direction.

body.text: Nobody wants to Google this. You want to be the kind of person whose back simply works, like a screen door or a golden retriever. Instead you are here, sitting slightly sideways, wondering why something that is supposed to be good leaves you walking like a question mark the next day. First: you are not broken, and you did not do anything wrong. A back that hurts after sex is doing the same thing a back does after a long drive or an afternoon of yard work. It is sending you a bill for a posture you held too long.
Why your back waits until morning to complain

Pain that hits during is one thing. Pain that shows up after, or the next morning, is usually a cumulative-load story. The tissue did not snap. It got loaded at the very end of its range, held there, and asked to do that for a while. Three usual suspects: too much time in a held arch or a held round, the partner on top holding a plank or letting the hips sag, and thrusting that comes from the low back instead of the hips. None of that feels dramatic in the moment. That is exactly the problem. The bill arrives later, with interest, around 7 a.m.

Here is the part nobody connects for you, because back-care lives in one silo and anything to do with the bedroom lives in another, and the two are apparently not allowed to talk. Backs tend to have a direction they hate. Some flare when they round forward, which is the flexion-intolerant crowd, the same people who suffer in low chairs and long car rides. Others flare when they arch back, the extension-intolerant crowd, who hate lying on their stomach and standing around at parties. Most morning-after flares come down to which direction got loaded, in what shape, and for how long. Figure out your direction and half the mystery evaporates.

Quick gut check: do you feel worse bending to tie your shoes, or worse leaning back to look at the ceiling? Worse forward usually means you flare on rounding. Worse backward usually means you flare on arching. That one answer changes which positions are friendly to you.
The fixes are boring, and they work

Nobody is going to sell you a candle for this, which is probably why no one talks about it. The real fixes are unglamorous. Keep the spine closer to neutral instead of parking it at the edge. Drive from the hips, not the low back, the way a deadlift should look and almost never does. Prop the pelvis with a pillow or a cushion so the shape you fall into is the kind one rather than the cruel one. Shorten the range a little; you lose nothing that matters. And do the daily endurance work so your back simply tolerates more, because a back with stamina forgives a multitude of sins.

items: Pick positions for your direction. Sidorkewicz and McGill (2014) actually ranked common positions by spine load and found the best pick depends on which direction you flare and whether you are the giver or the receiver. There is no single right answer, only the right one for you.
Prop the pelvis. A pillow under the hips or the low back turns an end-range arch or round into something closer to neutral. Free, and quietly one of the most effective things you can do.
Hinge from the hips. If the movement is coming from your waist, it is the wrong joint. The hips are built for this. The low back is not auditioning for the role.
Shorten the range. End range is where the load concentrates and where most morning-after flares are born. Back off a few degrees and let your hips cover the difference.
Build the base. The McGill Big 3 is endurance over range: it teaches the back to hold neutral under load. Boring, daily, and the reason a resilient back stops keeping score.

body.text: When it is not a pillow problem

Most of this is load and posture, and most of it gets better when you change the load and the posture. But a few things are not a propping problem and deserve an actual clinician, not another nine-dollar gummy or an MRI nobody bothers to explain to you. New pain that travels down a leg, numbness or pins and needles, or any change in bladder or bowel control is a see-someone signal. That is not us being cautious to cover ourselves. That is the one paragraph in this article we mean literally.

Everything else? It is workable, often this week. You do not need to give anything up or resign yourself to bracing for the morning after. You need to know your direction, change a couple of shapes, prop the right spot, and put a little stamina in the bank. That is the whole protocol. It is less exciting than a ninety-dollar pillow and considerably more likely to work.

faqs.q: Why does my back hurt after sex but feel fine during?
faqs.a: Because it is a cumulative-load thing, not an injury thing. Your back tolerated an end-range shape in the moment and sent the bill later. The fix is less time at the edge of your range, more drive from the hips, and a pillow under the pelvis to keep things closer to neutral.

faqs.q: Which positions are best for a bad lower back?
faqs.a: It depends on your direction and your role, which is the answer nobody likes but the honest one. Sidorkewicz and McGill (2014) ranked positions by spine load and showed the best choice changes based on whether you flare on rounding or arching, and whether you are giving or receiving. Our free ranker sorts this in two questions.

faqs.q: Is it bad to keep having sex if my back hurts after?
faqs.a: Usually not, as long as you do not have leg symptoms, numbness, or bladder or bowel changes. For ordinary load-and-posture soreness, the move is to modify (neutral spine, hip drive, propped pelvis, shorter range), not to quit. Quitting fixes nothing and resolves nothing.

faqs.q: How do I know if I flare on rounding or on arching?
faqs.a: Notice which everyday positions bother you. If sitting low, slouching, and tying your shoes feel worse, you likely flare on flexion (rounding). If arching, lying face down, and standing around feel worse, you likely flare on extension (arching). Then pick the shapes that go the other way.

faqs.q: When should I see a doctor about back pain after sex?
faqs.a: If you have new pain shooting down a leg, numbness or tingling, weakness, or any change in bladder or bowel control, see a clinician. Those are not posture problems and not something a pillow or a gummy will fix.

sources: Sidorkewicz NM, McGill SM. Documenting the typical lumbar spine motion during sexual activity. Eur Spine J. 2014.
Sidorkewicz NM, McGill SM. Male spine motion during coitus: implications for the low back pain patient. Spine (Phila Pa 1976). 2014.
McGill SM. Low Back Disorders: Evidence-Based Prevention and Rehabilitation. Human Kinetics.
rankerCta: Two questions, no MRI, no candle: take the free ranker and find the positions your specific back will actually forgive.
